summary: Julia Evans shares her early thinking about marketing for her zines business, exploring how to promote her work in a way that feels authentic and not annoying. She examines common internet marketing techniques like mailing lists, social proof, and advertising, evaluating each against her personal comfort level. Her current approach relies heavily on Twitter, where she posts free comics that naturally attract followers who then buy her zines. She considers whether to invest in email marketing and conversion tracking but remains skeptical of aggressive optimization, preferring to focus on making good things and letting people know where to find them.
key_points: Good marketing is fundamentally about clearly explaining what something is and why it's good, then letting people decide
Twitter drives roughly 70% of her zine sales through a natural cycle: post free comics, gain followers, announce paid zines
She's skeptical of conversion rate optimization, arguing it creates a rabbit hole that distracts from making great work
The key marketing insight she discovered is that people actually want to buy good things — you just need to tell them where
She prefers marketing approaches that balance discoverability with not being annoying, rejecting aggressive popups and social proof tactics
money_quotes: So basically as far as I can tell good marketing is just explaining what the thing is and why it is good in a clear way.
I think a marketing secret is 'people actually want to buy things that are good, it is useful to tell people where they can buy things that are good'.

Doing a bunch of work to track conversion rates feels bad to me: it seems like it would be really easy to go down a dumb rabbit hole of 'oh, let's try to increase conversion by 5%' instead of just focusing on making really good and cool things.
Obviously the most important thing in all of this is to make cool things that are useful to people, but I think cool useful writing does not actually sell itself!
Maybe marketing is all about 'make your things discoverable without being annoying'?
I don't try to hack/optimize this really: I just post comics when I make them and I try to make them good.
Whenever people refer to my writing as 'content' I get grumpy.
key_insight: The best marketing strategy for independent creators is making genuinely good work, sharing it freely, and simply telling people where they can buy more — no aggressive optimization needed.
